title: Faster matrix product state preparation by exploiting symmetry-induced block-sparsity
abstract: Matrix product states (MPS) serve as a key tool for studying quantum systems from chemistry and condensed-matter physics, making their preparation on quantum computers an important task in interfacing classical and quantum simulation. Many systems of interest have $U(1)$-symmetries induced by particle number and spin projection conservation, allowing to restrict the MPS tensors to be of block-sparse form, a property widely used in the implementation of classical algorithms such as the density matrix renormalization group. We reduce the cost of fault-tolerantly preparing block-sparse MPS within the standard ancilla-assisted linear-depth approach by implementing row and column permutations that transform the block-sparse matrices into block-diagonal form. These block-diagonal unitaries are then implemented via unitary synthesis, with the cost being determined by the size of the largest block. In this context, we modify the unitary synthesis approach of Berry et al. in order to reduce the Toffoli cost for real-valued unitaries by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. In numerical benchmarks, we achieve Toffoli cost improvement factors of $10 - 30$ compared to the state-of-the-art for MPS of various molecular systems.
summary: The authors present a more efficient way to prepare Matrix Product States on quantum computers by exploiting the block-sparse structure created by physical symmetries. By transforming these sparse matrices into block-diagonal form, they significantly reduce the number of required quantum gates. This leads to a massive reduction in computational resources for simulating complex molecular systems.
main_problem: The high computational cost of preparing Matrix Product States (MPS) on fault-tolerant quantum computers, specifically regarding the large number of Toffoli gates and qubits required.
main_result: Achieved a 10-30x reduction in Toffoli gate costs by exploiting block-sparsity from U(1) symmetries and a sqrt(2) reduction in cost for real-valued unitary synthesis.
method: An ancilla-assisted linear-depth approach using row and column permutations to transform block-sparse matrices into block-diagonal form, implemented via modified unitary synthesis and Givens rotations.
model_or_system: Fermionic many-body systems with U(1) symmetries (particle number and spin projection conservation), including molecular systems like P450 enzymes and Iron-Sulfur clusters.
key_observables: Toffoli gate count, qubit count, and state overlap (fidelity) with the target MPS.
important_parameters: Bond dimension (chi), site dimension (D), and the trade-off parameter (Lambda) controlling the ancilla vs. Toffoli gate balance.
main_assumptions: Assumes the MPS is in right-canonical form, all non-trivial bond dimensions are equal, and that the system possesses global Abelian symmetries.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the MPS preparation circuit; Figure 2 depicts the quantum circuit for implementing rotation layers; Figure 3 illustrates the transformation of block-sparse unitaries into block-diagonal form; Figure 4 shows the implementation of permutations using QROAM.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MPS preparation, details the symmetry-based block-diagonalization method, describes the modified unitary synthesis for real-valued unitaries, provides resource estimation via Qualtran, and concludes with numerical benchmarks on molecular systems.
why_interesting: This work provides a significant algorithmic optimization for the interface between classical tensor network methods (DMRG) and fault-tolerant quantum computing, which is crucial for future quantum chemistry simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: Circuit for implementing a layer of Rz/Ry rotations in (4) of the forms (2) on four qubits up to sign flips from X-measurements MX of the angle registers and the QROAM junk registers. The measurement results of the angle regis- ters are mx1 and mx2. We do not show the QROAM junk registers. The bottom two registers are the ancilla angle reg- isters into which the rotation angles are loaded via QROAM. The rotations use a phase-gradient state |ψ⟩as a resource state together with the loaded angles. Figure 3: Steps for transforming and extending the block-sparse matrix U ′ := U ′ i, represented by the blue part in the leftmost box, into a block-diagonal unitary V using permutation matrices. The content of the red rectangles R, which are put into the initially unspecified part ∗of the matrix in the second step, is chosen such that the emerging square blocks are unitary.

Figure 5: Decomposition of Ui =  U ′ i ∗ into unitaries on smaller subspaces with V moved to the prior site. The ancilla register has size w := log(m). For readability, we move the second qubit of the two-qubit site register below the ancilla register, yielding the order (site[0], anc, site[1]). In the matrix representations, it is assumed to be above, i.e., (site, anc). The individual unitaries are implemented up to sign flips, which are then fixed at the end.
